flashcards.front: What is the IIA"s definition of Internal Auditing?
flashcards.back: Internal auditing is an independent, objective assurance and consulting activity designed to add value and improve an organization"s operations. It helps an organization accomplish its objectives by bringing a systematic, disciplined approach to evaluate and improve the effectiveness of risk management, control, and governance processes.

flashcards.front: What is organizational independence in internal auditing?
flashcards.back: Organizational independence is the freedom from conditions that threaten the internal audit activity"s ability to carry out its responsibilities in an unbiased manner. It requires the CAE to report functionally to the board and have direct and unrestricted access to senior management.

flashcards.front: What is individual objectivity?
flashcards.back: Individual objectivity is an unbiased mental attitude that allows internal auditors to perform engagements without quality compromises. Auditors must not subordinate their judgment to others and must avoid conflicts of interest.

flashcards.front: What is due professional care?
flashcards.back: Due professional care is the care and skill expected of a reasonably prudent and competent internal auditor. It does NOT imply infallibility but requires consideration of: extent of work needed, complexity, significance of matters, adequacy of governance/risk/control, and probability of errors or fraud.

flashcards.front: What are the four principles of the IIA Code of Ethics? (Mnemonic)
flashcards.back:
**"IOCO"**

• **I**ntegrity - Establishes trust and basis for reliance
• **O**bjectivity - Exhibit highest professional objectivity
• **C**onfidentiality - Respect value and ownership of information
• **C**ompetency - Apply knowledge, skills, and experience

flashcards.front: What are the key responsibilities of the board regarding internal audit?
flashcards.back:
• Approve the internal audit charter
• Approve the risk-based audit plan
• Approve the internal audit budget and resource plan
• Receive communications from the CAE on audit activity performance
• Make appropriate inquiries about audit findings
• Approve decisions regarding CAE appointment and removal

flashcards.front: What must the internal audit charter include?
flashcards.back:
The internal audit charter must define:

• Purpose, authority, and responsibility of the internal audit activity
• Position of internal audit within the organization
• Functional reporting relationship to the board
• Authorization for access to records, personnel, and properties
• Scope of internal audit activities
• Nature of assurance and consulting services

flashcards.front: What is the difference between assurance and consulting services?
flashcards.back:
**Assurance Services:**
• Objective examination of evidence
• Independent assessment provided to management/board
• Three parties: auditor, process owner, users
• Examples: financial, compliance, operations audits

**Consulting Services:**
• Advisory in nature
• Agreed upon with client
• Two parties: auditor and client
• Examples: training, advice, facilitation

Nature and scope determined by client for consulting vs. auditor for assurance.

flashcards.front: What factors should the CAE consider in developing the risk-based audit plan?
flashcards.back:
• Organization"s risk management framework
• Significant risks to objectives and operations
• Input from senior management and the board
• Prior audit results and known issues
• Legal/regulatory requirements
• Expected changes in business conditions
• IT and business system changes
• Emerging issues and industry developments
• Resource requirements and constraints

flashcards.front: What is QAIP and what are its components?
flashcards.back:
**Quality Assurance and Improvement Program (QAIP):**

Must include both internal and external assessments:

**Internal Assessments:**
• Ongoing monitoring (continuous)
• Periodic self-assessments

**External Assessments:**
• At least once every FIVE years
• By qualified, independent assessor or team
• CAE discusses requirements with board

Results communicated to board with action plans.

flashcards.front: What are the key elements of engagement planning?
flashcards.back:
**Engagement Objectives:** What the engagement intends to accomplish

**Scope:** Activities, timeframe, and limitations

**Resources:** Team composition, skills, knowledge

**Work Program:** Procedures for identifying, analyzing, evaluating, and documenting information

**Risk Assessment:** Identify significant risks and tailor work accordingly

flashcards.front: What is the definition of control in internal auditing?
flashcards.back:
Control is any action taken by management, the board, and other parties to manage risk and increase the likelihood that established objectives and goals will be achieved.

Management plans, organizes, and directs the performance of sufficient actions to provide reasonable assurance that objectives will be achieved.

flashcards.front: What are the three categories of controls by timing? (Mnemonic)
flashcards.back:
**"PDC" - like Photo, Document, Check**

• **P**reventive - Stop errors/fraud before they occur
  (Segregation of duties, authorization, access controls)

• **D**etective - Identify errors/fraud that have occurred
  (Reconciliations, reviews, exception reports)

• **C**orrective - Fix errors/fraud that have been detected
  (Backup restoration, error correction procedures)

flashcards.front: What are the attributes of audit evidence? (SCAR)
flashcards.back:
**S**ufficient - Enough evidence to support conclusions

**C**ompetent - Reliable and relevant

**A**ppropriate - Relevant to audit objectives

**R**eliable - Based on source and nature

Evidence obtained directly by auditor > indirectly obtained
Original documents > copies
Independent sources > auditee sources

flashcards.front: Compare statistical and non-statistical (judgmental) sampling.
flashcards.back:
**Statistical Sampling:**
• Random selection
• Quantitative conclusions about population
• Measurable sampling risk
• Results can be projected
• Less auditor judgment in selection

**Non-Statistical (Judgmental):**
• Items selected using judgment
• Cannot project results statistically
• Sampling risk not quantified
• May be appropriate for small populations
• More reliance on auditor expertise

flashcards.front: What must be included in engagement communications?
flashcards.back:
**Required Elements:**
• Objectives and scope
• Applicable conclusions
• Recommendations and action plans

**For significant issues:**
• **C**ondition - What exists (finding)
• **C**riteria - What should exist (standard)
• **C**ause - Why it happened
• **C**onsequence - Risk/impact (effect)

**Quality criteria:** Accurate, objective, clear, concise, constructive, complete, timely

flashcards.front: What are the components of an audit finding? (4 Cs)
flashcards.back:
**The 4 Cs of Audit Findings:**

• **C**ondition - What is (current state/finding)
• **C**riteria - What should be (standard/policy/law)
• **C**ause - Why the gap exists (root cause)
• **C**onsequence - What\

flashcards.front: What are the CAE\
flashcards.back:
The CAE must establish a follow-up process to monitor and ensure that:

• Management actions have been effectively implemented, OR
• Senior management has accepted the risk of not taking action

The internal audit activity must monitor disposition of results and determine if senior management has accepted the risk of not taking corrective action.

If CAE believes risk is unacceptable, must discuss with senior management and potentially the board.

flashcards.front: What is internal audit\
flashcards.back:
Internal audit must assess and make appropriate recommendations for improving the governance process to:

• Promote ethics and values
• Ensure effective performance management and accountability
• Communicate risk and control information
• Coordinate activities between board, external/internal auditors, and management

flashcards.front: What is risk and what is risk management?
flashcards.back: **Risk:** The possibility of an event occurring that will have an impact on the achievement of objectives. Risk is measured in terms of impact and likelihood.\n\n**Risk Management:** A process to identify, assess, manage, and control potential events or situations to provide reasonable assurance regarding achievement of objectives.

flashcards.front: What are examples of impairments to independence or objectivity?
flashcards.back: Personal conflicts of interest, scope limitations, restrictions on access, resource limitations, management influence over engagement conclusions, financial interests in the audited operation, prior operational responsibility for audited area, close personal relationships.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between risk appetite and risk tolerance?
flashcards.back: Risk Appetite: The broad level of risk an organization is willing to accept in pursuit of its objectives (board-level, strategic). Risk Tolerance: The acceptable level of variation relative to achievement of a specific objective (operational, measurable). Risk tolerance is narrower and more specific than risk appetite.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between preventive, detective, and corrective controls?
flashcards.back: Preventive: Stop unwanted events before they occur (approvals, segregation, access controls). Detective: Identify unwanted events after they occur (reconciliations, audits, exception reports). Corrective: Fix problems after detection (backup restoration, disciplinary action, process changes).

flashcards.front: What elements must be included in the engagement work program?
flashcards.back: The work program must include: 1) Objectives of the engagement, 2) Procedures for identifying, analyzing, evaluating, and documenting information, 3) Procedures must be sufficient to achieve engagement objectives, 4) Must be approved prior to implementation, 5) Any adjustments approved promptly.

flashcards.front: What are the attributes of reliable audit evidence?
flashcards.back: SRRC: Sufficient (adequate quantity), Reliable (trustworthy, verified), Relevant (pertains to audit objectives), Competent (valid, factual). Evidence should support engagement observations and conclusions. Physical evidence and external confirmations are generally most reliable.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between attribute sampling and variable sampling?
flashcards.back: Attribute Sampling: Tests yes/no characteristics (e.g., was approval obtained?). Used for compliance testing. Measures rate of occurrence. Variable Sampling: Tests numerical values (e.g., invoice amounts). Used for substantive testing. Estimates population values or totals.

flashcards.front: What must be included in engagement communications?
flashcards.back: Communications must include: 1) Objectives and scope, 2) Conclusions, 3) Recommendations and action plans, 4) Opinion when appropriate. Must be accurate, objective, clear, concise, constructive, complete, and timely. Significant errors must be corrected and communicated.

flashcards.front: What are the five elements of an audit finding?
flashcards.back: CCCER: Condition (what is), Criteria (what should be), Cause (why it happened), Effect/Impact (significance), Recommendation (what to do). A complete finding addresses all five elements to support conclusions and enable corrective action.

flashcards.front: What does "Conforms with the IIA Standards" mean?
flashcards.back: Means the internal audit activity and individual auditors demonstrate achievement of all applicable Standards. Results from effective implementation of the Mandatory Guidance (Definition, Principles, Standards, Code of Ethics). External assessments required at least every 5 years.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between inherent risk and residual risk?
flashcards.back: Inherent Risk: The risk before any controls are applied. The natural risk level in absence of management action. Residual Risk: The risk remaining after controls are applied. Should be within risk appetite. Formula: Residual = Inherent - Control Effectiveness.

flashcards.front: What are the CAE"s responsibilities for follow-up?
flashcards.back: The CAE must establish and maintain a system to monitor the disposition of results communicated. Must establish a follow-up process to monitor and ensure management actions have been effectively implemented or that senior management has accepted the risk of not taking action.

flashcards.front: What does functional reporting to the board mean?
flashcards.back: Functional reporting to the board includes: 1) Approving the internal audit charter, 2) Approving the risk-based audit plan, 3) Approving the CAE appointment and removal, 4) Approving the CAE remuneration, 5) Receiving communications from the CAE on results, 6) Approving the budget and resource plan.

flashcards.front: What are internal auditors\
flashcards.back: Auditors must: 1) Be prudent in use and protection of information, 2) Not use information for personal gain, 3) Not use information in a manner contrary to law or detrimental to organization, 4) Protect information even after leaving employment. May disclose when required by law or professional standards.

flashcards.front: When must the CAE disclose nonconformance with the Standards?
flashcards.back: When nonconformance impacts the overall scope or operation of the internal audit activity. Must disclose to senior management and the board. Individual engagement nonconformance must be disclosed in engagement communications. Must identify affected Standard(s) and reason for nonconformance.

flashcards.front: What are the four risk response strategies?
flashcards.back: ARAT: Accept (tolerate the risk, within appetite), Reduce/Mitigate (implement controls), Avoid (eliminate the activity causing risk), Transfer (insurance, outsourcing, contracts). Selection depends on risk appetite, cost-benefit analysis, and strategic objectives.

flashcards.front: What is "tone at the top" and why is it important?
flashcards.back: Tone at the top refers to the ethical climate and attitude of senior management and the board toward integrity, ethics, and compliance. It sets the cultural foundation for the organization. A positive tone promotes ethical behavior; a weak tone creates opportunities for misconduct. Internal audit assesses and reports on this.

flashcards.front: What must internal audit evaluate regarding governance processes?
flashcards.back: Internal audit must evaluate: 1) Decision-making and oversight, 2) Ethics and values, 3) Performance management, 4) Risk and control information, 5) Coordination of activities with external assurance providers. Make recommendations for improvement where needed.

flashcards.front: What are the components of the control environment (COSO)?
flashcards.back: The control environment sets the tone for the organization. Components: 1) Integrity and ethical values, 2) Board oversight responsibility, 3) Organizational structure, 4) Commitment to competence, 5) Accountability. It is the foundation of internal control.

flashcards.front: What are the five components of the COSO Internal Control Framework?
flashcards.back: CRIME: Control Environment (foundation), Risk Assessment (identify threats), Information & Communication (relevant info), Monitoring Activities (ongoing evaluation), Existing Control Activities (policies and procedures). Applies across operations, reporting, and compliance objectives.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between IT General Controls and Application Controls?
flashcards.back: ITGCs: Controls over IT environment and infrastructure (access, change management, operations, SDLC). Apply across all systems. Application Controls: Controls specific to an application (input validation, processing, output). ITGCs support application control effectiveness.

flashcards.front: What is internal audit\
flashcards.back: Internal audit should: 1) Evaluate compliance with laws, regulations, and policies, 2) Assess adequacy of compliance programs, 3) Report compliance issues to management and board, 4) NOT own compliance (management responsibility), 5) Provide independent assurance on compliance effectiveness.

flashcards.front: What are the requirements for external quality assessments?
flashcards.back: Must be conducted at least every 5 years by a qualified, independent assessor or team. Can be full external or self-assessment with independent external validation. CAE must discuss form and frequency with board. Results and action plans communicated to senior management and board.

flashcards.front: What is internal audit\
flashcards.back: Internal audit should: 1) Understand fraud risks, 2) Evaluate controls designed to prevent/detect fraud, 3) Be alert to fraud indicators during engagements, 4) NOT have primary responsibility (management\

flashcards.front: What is an audit universe?
flashcards.back: The audit universe is a comprehensive list of all auditable entities or activities within the organization. Includes business units, processes, projects, systems, and compliance areas. Serves as the foundation for risk-based audit planning. Should be updated periodically as organization changes.

flashcards.front: How should the CAE manage internal audit resources?
flashcards.back: CAE must ensure: 1) Appropriate, sufficient, and effectively deployed resources, 2) Resources match engagement requirements, 3) Knowledge, skills, and competencies available (internally or externally), 4) Resource plan communicated to senior management and board for review and approval.

flashcards.front: How should internal audit coordinate with external auditors?
flashcards.back: Coordination should include: 1) Sharing of work plans and schedules, 2) Access to each other\

flashcards.front: What are the IIA Rules of Conduct for Competency?
flashcards.back: Internal auditors shall: 1) Engage only in services for which they have necessary KSAs, 2) Perform services in accordance with Standards, 3) Continually improve proficiency, effectiveness, and quality. Related to the Principle of Competency in the Code of Ethics.

flashcards.front: What should the CAE communicate to senior management and the board?
flashcards.back: The CAE should report on: 1) Audit plan and resource needs, 2) Significant risk exposures and control issues, 3) Audit results and recommendations, 4) Quality assurance and improvement program results, 5) Conformance with the Code of Ethics and Standards, 6) Any impairments to independence.

flashcards.front: What is the Mission of Internal Auditing per the IPPF?
flashcards.back: To enhance and protect organizational value by providing risk-based and objective assurance, advice, and insight.

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1000 - Purpose, Authority, and Responsibility?
flashcards.back: The purpose, authority, and responsibility of the internal audit activity must be formally defined in an internal audit charter, consistent with the Mission and the mandatory elements of the IPPF.

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1010 - Recognizing Mandatory Guidance?
flashcards.back: The CAE must discuss the Mission, Core Principles, Definition, Code of Ethics, and Standards with senior management and the board.

flashcards.front: What are the 10 Core Principles for the Professional Practice of Internal Auditing?
flashcards.back:
1. Demonstrates integrity
2. Demonstrates competence and due professional care
3. Is objective and free from undue influence
4. Aligns with strategies, objectives, and risks
5. Is appropriately positioned and adequately resourced
6. Demonstrates quality and continuous improvement
7. Communicates effectively
8. Provides risk-based assurance
9. Is insightful, proactive, and future-focused
10. Promotes organizational improvement

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1110 - Organizational Independence?
flashcards.back: The CAE must report to a level within the organization that allows the internal audit activity to fulfill its responsibilities. The CAE must confirm to the board, at least annually, organizational independence.

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1120 - Individual Objectivity?
flashcards.back: Internal auditors must have an impartial, unbiased attitude and avoid any conflict of interest.

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1130 - Impairment to Independence or Objectivity?
flashcards.back: If independence or objectivity is impaired in fact or appearance, the details must be disclosed. The nature of disclosure depends on the impairment.

flashcards.front: What safeguards can address threats to independence and objectivity?
flashcards.back: Staff rotation, supervision and review, alternate assignment of engagement responsibilities, policies prohibiting gifts/inducements, cooling-off periods, disclosure requirements, training and awareness programs.

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1200 - Proficiency and Due Professional Care?
flashcards.back: Engagements must be performed with proficiency and due professional care.

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1210 - Proficiency?
flashcards.back: Internal auditors must possess the knowledge, skills, and other competencies needed to perform their individual responsibilities. The internal audit activity collectively must possess or obtain the competencies needed.

flashcards.front: What knowledge should internal auditors possess?
flashcards.back: Risk management, control frameworks, governance principles, relevant legislation/regulation, IT concepts and emerging technology, fraud detection, data analytics, industry-specific knowledge, financial and management accounting.

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1220 - Due Professional Care?
flashcards.back: Internal auditors must apply the care and skill expected of a reasonably prudent and competent internal auditor. Due professional care does not imply infallibility.

flashcards.front: What must internal auditors consider when exercising due professional care?
flashcards.back: Extent of work needed, relative complexity and materiality, adequacy of governance/risk management/controls, probability of significant errors/fraud/noncompliance, cost of assurance vs. potential benefits.

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1230 - Continuing Professional Development?
flashcards.back: Internal auditors must enhance their knowledge, skills, and other competencies through continuing professional development.

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1310 - Requirements of the Quality Assurance and Improvement Program?
flashcards.back: The QAIP must include both internal and external assessments.

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1311 - Internal Assessments?
flashcards.back: Internal assessments must include ongoing monitoring of internal audit activity performance and periodic self-assessments or assessments by other persons with sufficient knowledge of internal audit practices.

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1312 - External Assessments?
flashcards.back: External assessments must be conducted at least once every five years by a qualified, independent assessor or assessment team from outside the organization.

flashcards.front: What qualifies an external assessor for QAIP?
flashcards.back: Must be qualified (competent in internal audit profession and external assessment process), independent (no real or apparent conflict of interest, not part of or under control of the organization).

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1320 - Reporting on the QAIP?
flashcards.back: The CAE must communicate the results of the QAIP to senior management and the board. Disclosure should include scope and frequency, qualifications and independence of assessor(s), conclusions, and corrective action plans.

flashcards.front: When can "Conforms with the International Standards" be used?
flashcards.back: Only when results of the QAIP support this statement. Use of this phrase is appropriate when the internal audit activity meets the expectations detailed in the Definition, Code of Ethics, and Standards.

flashcards.front: What is Standard 1322 - Disclosure of Nonconformance?
flashcards.back: When nonconformance impacts the overall scope or operation of internal audit activity, the CAE must disclose to senior management and board.

flashcards.front: What are the Rules of Conduct for Integrity?
flashcards.back: Shall perform work with honesty, diligence, and responsibility. Shall observe the law and make disclosures expected by law. Shall not knowingly be a party to illegal activity or discrediting conduct. Shall respect and contribute to legitimate organizational objectives.

flashcards.front: What are the Rules of Conduct for Objectivity?
flashcards.back: Shall not participate in activities that would impair judgment. Shall not accept anything that would impair judgment. Shall disclose all material facts known.

flashcards.front: What are the Rules of Conduct for Confidentiality?
flashcards.back: Shall be prudent in use and protection of information. Shall not use information for personal gain or contrary to law or organizational objectives.

flashcards.front: What are the Rules of Conduct for Competency?
flashcards.back: Shall engage only in those services for which they have necessary knowledge, skills, and experience. Shall perform internal audit services in accordance with the Standards. Shall continually improve proficiency, effectiveness, and quality of services.

flashcards.front: What is governance as defined in the IPPF?
flashcards.back: The combination of processes and structures implemented by the board to inform, direct, manage, and monitor the activities of the organization toward the achievement of its objectives.

flashcards.front: What is risk as defined in the IPPF?
flashcards.back: The possibility of an event occurring that will have an impact on the achievement of objectives. Risk is measured in terms of impact and likelihood.

flashcards.front: What is the Three Lines Model?
flashcards.back:
First Line: Operational management owns and manages risks
Second Line: Risk management, compliance, oversight functions
Third Line: Internal audit provides independent assurance
Governing body and senior management are outside the three lines.

flashcards.front: What are the responsibilities of the First Line?
flashcards.back: Provide products/services to clients, manage risk, maintain effective internal controls, achieve objectives. Directly accountable to governing body for risk decisions and actions.

flashcards.front: What are the responsibilities of the Second Line?
flashcards.back: Provide expertise, support, monitoring, challenge on risk-related matters. Develops policies, provides training, monitors compliance, reports on risk issues. May include risk management, compliance, quality, security, fraud prevention.

flashcards.front: What are the responsibilities of the Third Line?
flashcards.back: Provide independent, objective assurance and advice on all matters related to governance, risk management, and control. Must be independent from management to preserve objectivity.

flashcards.front: What is an assurance engagement?
flashcards.back: An objective examination of evidence for the purpose of providing an independent assessment on governance, risk management, and control processes. Examples: financial, performance, compliance, system security, due diligence engagements.

flashcards.front: What is a consulting engagement?
flashcards.back: Advisory and related client service activities whose nature and scope are agreed with the client, intended to add value and improve governance, risk management, and control processes without assuming management responsibility.

flashcards.front: What are common fraud red flags (indicators)?
flashcards.back: Lifestyle beyond means, financial difficulties, unusually close vendor relationships, control weaknesses, anomalies in data/transactions, lack of segregation of duties, lack of management oversight, unusual journal entries, missing documentation.

flashcards.front: What is the internal auditor"s responsibility regarding fraud?
flashcards.back: Must have sufficient knowledge to evaluate risk of fraud and how organization manages fraud risk. Not expected to have expertise of person with primary fraud responsibility. Must consider probability of significant errors, fraud, or noncompliance (Standard 1220).

flashcards.front: What is Implementation Standard 1010.A1?
flashcards.back: The CAE must discuss the internal audit activity"s plans and resource requirements, including significant interim changes, with senior management and the board. This includes resource needs, any limitations, and their impact.

flashcards.front: What are the four categories of the IPPF Mandatory Guidance?
flashcards.back:
1. Core Principles for the Professional Practice of Internal Auditing
2. Definition of Internal Auditing
3. Code of Ethics
4. Standards (including Attribute, Performance, and Implementation Standards)

flashcards.front: What constitutes Recommended Guidance in the IPPF?
flashcards.back:
Implementation Guides: Assist in applying Standards
Supplemental Guidance: Additional detailed guidance (Practice Guides, Global Technology Audit Guides)

Recommended guidance is not mandatory but endorsed by IIA.

flashcards.front: What factors support the organizational status of internal audit?
flashcards.back: Functional reporting to board/audit committee, dual reporting (administrative to senior management), CAE meets privately with board, board approves charter/plan/budget, board involved in CAE appointment/removal/compensation.

flashcards.front: What is the board"s role in supporting internal audit independence?
flashcards.back: Approve internal audit charter, approve risk-based audit plan, approve budget and resource plan, receive communications from CAE, monitor CAE access and support, participate in CAE evaluation, ensure CAE has unrestricted access.

flashcards.front: How do independence requirements differ for assurance vs consulting?
flashcards.back: Assurance: Must be independent of the area being audited; cannot assume management responsibilities in audited area\n\nConsulting: May provide services requested by management even if it limits independence, but must disclose any impairments; nature of work disclosed in report.

flashcards.front: What does Standard 1220 require regarding due professional care?
flashcards.back: Internal auditors must apply care and skill expected of a reasonably prudent and competent auditor. Consider: Extent of work needed, complexity/materiality, adequacy of governance/risk/controls, probability of errors/fraud, cost vs. benefit.

flashcards.front: What is required for collective proficiency (Standard 1210)?
flashcards.back: Internal audit activity must collectively possess knowledge, skills, and competencies needed to perform responsibilities. CAE obtains competent advice if lacking expertise internally. Includes IT knowledge and fraud awareness.

flashcards.front: What qualifications are required for external quality assessment team?
flashcards.back: Qualified, independent, competent in professional practice of internal auditing. Competence demonstrated through experience and qualifications. Must be free of actual or potential conflicts of interest. Team leader should be CIA/equivalent.

flashcards.front: What are the approaches for external quality assessment?
flashcards.back:
Full External Assessment: Team of qualified external assessors

Self-Assessment with Independent Validation: Internal self-assessment validated by qualified external assessor

Both approaches must evaluate conformance with IPPF.

flashcards.front: What are the key board committees and their functions?
flashcards.back:
Audit Committee: Financial reporting, internal controls, internal/external audit
Risk Committee: Risk oversight and ERM
Nomination Committee: Board composition, succession
Compensation Committee: Executive compensation
Governance Committee: Board practices, policies

flashcards.front: What are the key audit committee responsibilities?
flashcards.back: Oversee financial reporting process, review financial statements, oversee internal and external auditors, review internal controls, discuss risk management, receive reports on fraud/whistleblower matters, approve non-audit services.

flashcards.front: What elements demonstrate "tone at the top"?
flashcards.back: Ethical leadership and behavior, clear communication of values, commitment to integrity, accountability, transparent decision-making, response to ethical violations, support for internal audit and compliance, whistleblower protection.

flashcards.front: What are the "5Cs" of good governance?
flashcards.back:
Commitment: Dedication to stakeholder interests
Capability: Skills and knowledge of board
Culture: Ethical tone and values
Communication: Transparent disclosure
Control: Effective oversight mechanisms

flashcards.front: Who are the key stakeholders in corporate governance?
flashcards.back:
Internal: Board, management, employees, internal audit
External: Shareholders, customers, suppliers, regulators, creditors, external auditors, communities, society

Governance balances interests of multiple stakeholders.

flashcards.front: What are the major categories of organizational risk?
flashcards.back:
Strategic: Business model, competitive, market risks
Operational: Process, people, technology, compliance
Financial: Liquidity, credit, market, currency
Hazard: Property, liability, personnel safety
Reputational: Brand, public perception, trust

flashcards.front: What are the components of risk assessment?
flashcards.back:
Risk Identification: Recognize risks to objectives
Risk Analysis: Assess likelihood and impact (inherent vs. residual)
Risk Evaluation: Compare against risk criteria/appetite
Risk Prioritization: Rank based on significance

flashcards.front: What are the risk response strategies (the 4Ts)?
flashcards.back:
Treat/Mitigate: Implement controls to reduce likelihood/impact
Transfer: Shift risk to third party (insurance, outsourcing)
Terminate/Avoid: Stop activity causing risk
Tolerate/Accept: Accept within risk appetite

Choice depends on cost-benefit analysis.

flashcards.front: What are emerging risks and how should they be managed?
flashcards.back: Emerging risks: New or evolving risks not yet fully understood (technology disruption, climate change, geopolitical, cyber threats)\n\nManagement: Environmental scanning, scenario analysis, early warning indicators, flexible response planning.

flashcards.front: How is risk quantified?
flashcards.back:
Risk = Likelihood × Impact

Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) = Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) × Annual Rate of Occurrence (ARO)

Risk Score = Inherent Risk - Control Effectiveness = Residual Risk

flashcards.front: What are the elements of the COSO control environment?
flashcards.back:
1. Integrity and ethical values
2. Board oversight
3. Organizational structure
4. Commitment to competence
5. Accountability

Control environment sets the tone and provides discipline/structure.

flashcards.front: What are common categories of control activities?
flashcards.back:
Preventive: Stop errors/fraud before they occur
Detective: Identify errors/fraud after they occur
Corrective: Fix problems after detection

Examples: Authorization, reconciliation, segregation of duties, physical controls, IT controls.

flashcards.front: What are the types of COSO monitoring activities?
flashcards.back:
Ongoing Monitoring: Built into normal operations (supervisory review, reconciliations, exception reports)

Separate Evaluations: Periodic assessments (internal audit, self-assessments)

Monitoring evaluates whether controls are present and functioning.

flashcards.front: What are the inherent limitations of internal control?
flashcards.back:
Human error and judgment, management override, collusion, cost-benefit constraints, changing conditions, unforeseen circumstances

Internal control provides reasonable but not absolute assurance.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between hard and soft controls?
flashcards.back:
Hard Controls: Tangible, documented controls (policies, procedures, segregation of duties, reconciliations, approvals)

Soft Controls: Intangible controls (culture, ethics, management style, communication, competence, morale)

Both types are essential for effective control.

flashcards.front: What are the three main categories of occupational fraud (per ACFE)?
flashcards.back:
Asset Misappropriation: Theft/misuse of assets (most common, lowest loss)

Corruption: Bribery, conflicts of interest, extortion

Financial Statement Fraud: Intentional misstatement (least common, highest loss)

flashcards.front: What are common methods to detect fraud?
flashcards.back: Tips/Hotlines (most effective), internal audit reviews, management review, account reconciliation, document examination, external audit, data analytics, surprise audits, surveillance, accidental discovery.

flashcards.front: What are common red flags indicating potential fraud?
flashcards.back: Behavioral: Living beyond means, financial difficulties, unusual behavior, control issues, irritability\n\nOrganizational: Weak internal controls, override of controls, inadequate segregation, lack of oversight, pressure to meet targets

flashcards.front: What are key anti-fraud controls?
flashcards.back: Strong tone at the top, code of conduct, fraud awareness training, anonymous reporting hotline, background checks, job rotation, mandatory vacations, segregation of duties, surprise audits, data analytics, management certification.

flashcards.front: What is the MICE acronym for fraud motivation?
flashcards.back:
Money: Financial need or greed
Ideology: Belief system justifies action
Coercion: Forced or blackmailed
Ego: Sense of superiority or entitlement

MICE provides alternative to Fraud Triangle for understanding motivation.

flashcards.front: How should internal and external auditors coordinate per Standard 2050?
flashcards.back: Share information, coordinate activities, avoid duplication, ensure adequate coverage. CAE should share audit plan, work papers, reports. Coordination may include: joint planning, reliance on work, access to each other\

flashcards.front: What are the options for outsourcing internal audit?
flashcards.back:
Full outsourcing: Entire function performed externally
Co-sourcing: Supplement internal resources with external
Guest auditor: Use of auditors from other parts of organization

CAE retains responsibility regardless of delivery model.

flashcards.front: What should the CAE report to senior management?
flashcards.back: Internal audit activity"s purpose, authority, responsibility, and performance relative to plan. Significant risk exposures and control issues. Resource requirements. Concerns regarding management or board acceptance of risks.

flashcards.front: What should the CAE communicate to the board?
flashcards.back: Results of engagements, significant risk exposures, internal control concerns, significant governance issues, audit plan and significant changes, resource adequacy, management"s action on high-risk matters, emerging issues.

flashcards.front: What communication skills are important for internal auditors?
flashcards.back: Active listening, clear writing, effective presentation, persuasion, interviewing, negotiation, facilitation, conflict resolution. Communications must be accurate, objective, clear, concise, constructive, complete, and timely.

flashcards.front: How do internal auditors apply critical thinking?
flashcards.back: Question assumptions, analyze evidence objectively, consider alternative explanations, identify logical fallacies, synthesize information, draw reasonable conclusions, maintain professional skepticism throughout the engagement.

flashcards.front: How should internal auditors handle ethical dilemmas?
flashcards.back: Identify the ethical issue, gather facts, consider stakeholders, evaluate alternatives against Code of Ethics, consult with others (supervisor, ethics hotline, legal), make decision, document reasoning, follow through with action.

flashcards.front: When must non-conformance with IPPF be disclosed?
flashcards.back: When non-conformance impacts the overall scope or operation of internal audit activity, the nature and impact must be disclosed to appropriate parties. Single standard deviation generally requires disclosure when it affects engagement conclusions.

flashcards.front: What are the key CAE responsibilities?
flashcards.back: Develop and maintain QAIP, ensure conformance with IPPF, develop risk-based audit plan, ensure adequate resources and expertise, communicate with board and management, coordinate with other assurance providers, develop staff.

flashcards.front: How does internal audit add value to the organization?
flashcards.back: Provide objective assurance on governance, risk, and controls. Identify improvement opportunities. Provide insights and recommendations. Facilitate risk assessment. Support strategic objectives. Enhance efficiency and effectiveness.

flashcards.front: How does Core Principle 3 "Demonstrates competence and due professional care" apply?
flashcards.back: Auditors must have requisite knowledge, skills, and experience. Apply due care expected of a reasonably prudent auditor. Pursue continuing professional development. Recognize and respect limits of knowledge.

flashcards.front: How does Core Principle 4 "Is quality driven" apply?
flashcards.back: Perform work in accordance with IPPF Standards. Maintain quality assurance and improvement program. Seek continuous improvement in efficiency and effectiveness. Provide constructive, value-added recommendations.

flashcards.front: How does Core Principle 5 "Communicates effectively" apply?
flashcards.back: Communications are accurate, objective, clear, concise, constructive, complete, and timely. Reports tailored to audience. Difficult messages delivered with tact and professionalism. Feedback sought and incorporated.

flashcards.front: How does Core Principle 6 "Provides risk-based assurance" apply?
flashcards.back: Audit plan based on risk assessment. Focus resources on significant risks. Consider risk in engagement planning and execution. Help organization understand and manage risks. Provide insights on risk management effectiveness.

flashcards.front: How does Core Principle 7 "Is insightful, proactive, and future-focused" apply?
flashcards.back: Anticipate future trends and emerging risks. Provide forward-looking recommendations. Identify root causes, not just symptoms. Share best practices across organization. Challenge status quo constructively.

flashcards.front: How does Core Principle 8 "Promotes organizational improvement" apply?
flashcards.back: Evaluate and improve effectiveness of governance, risk management, and controls. Recommend improvements to drive efficiency and effectiveness. Support organizational achievement of objectives. Foster culture of continuous improvement.

flashcards.front: How should the internal audit charter define scope?
flashcards.back: Defines unrestricted access to records, personnel, and physical properties. Establishes activities subject to audit. Includes authority to conduct any audit or review. Should allow sufficient flexibility for emerging risks.

flashcards.front: How often should the internal audit charter be reviewed?
flashcards.back: Periodically reviewed and updated (at least annually is best practice). Present to board for approval when changes made. Ensure alignment with IPPF, organizational changes, and evolving expectations.

flashcards.front: What makes an audit plan "risk-based" (Standard 2010)?
flashcards.back: Audit plan considers organization\

flashcards.front: How should the audit plan provide coverage over time?
flashcards.back:
High-risk areas: Annually or more frequently
Medium-risk: Every 2-3 years
Low-risk: Every 3-5 years or as needed

Consider cyclical coverage, regulatory requirements, and changes in risk profile.

flashcards.front: What is self-review threat to objectivity?
flashcards.back: Risk that auditors may not objectively evaluate things they previously worked on. Examples: Auditing system you helped implement, reviewing your own work, consulting then auditing same area. Mitigation: Waiting period, different team members.

flashcards.front: What is familiarity threat to objectivity?
flashcards.back: Risk that close relationships may impair judgment. Examples: Long-term assignment to same area, auditing close friends/relatives, personal relationships with auditees. Mitigation: Rotation, disclosure, supervision.

flashcards.front: What is advocacy threat to objectivity?
flashcards.back: Risk when auditor promotes a position or opinion to the point that objectivity is compromised. Examples: Promoting management\

flashcards.front: What is intimidation threat to objectivity?
flashcards.back: Risk that auditors may be influenced by threats or pressure. Examples: Management pressure to change findings, implied job consequences, aggressive behavior by auditees. Mitigation: Reporting channels to board, documented policies.

flashcards.front: What is professional skepticism and how is it applied?
flashcards.back: Questioning mind, alertness to conditions indicating possible misstatement or fraud. Critical assessment of evidence. Not accepting information at face value. Corroborating representations. Considering alternative explanations.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between professional skepticism and cynicism?
flashcards.back:
Skepticism: Objective questioning, seeks evidence, considers alternatives, maintains open mind, respects evidence

Cynicism: Assuming worst intentions, distrust regardless of evidence, negative attitude, closed minded

Skepticism is professional; cynicism damages relationships.

flashcards.front: What IT knowledge is required of internal auditors (Standard 1210.A3)?
flashcards.back: All internal auditors must have sufficient knowledge to evaluate risk exposures and control effectiveness related to IT-based systems. May need specialized IT audit resources for complex IT environments.

flashcards.front: What technology skills benefit internal auditors?
flashcards.back: Data analytics, audit management software, collaboration tools, visualization tools, process mining, RPA, working with APIs, understanding databases, cybersecurity awareness, emerging technology familiarity.

flashcards.front: What is combined assurance?
flashcards.back: Coordinating activities of all assurance providers (internal audit, external audit, management, risk, compliance, quality) to optimize risk and control assessment. Reduces gaps and duplication. CAE often coordinates.

flashcards.front: What is an assurance map?
flashcards.back: Visual tool showing key risks, controls, and assurance coverage. Identifies: Who provides assurance on each risk? What type of assurance? Any gaps or overlaps? Helps optimize assurance resources and coverage.

flashcards.front: How should internal audit manage stakeholder relationships?
flashcards.back: Understand stakeholder needs and expectations. Communicate regularly. Build trust through quality and professionalism. Manage conflicts appropriately. Balance independence with good working relationships. Seek feedback.

flashcards.front: How should auditors handle conflicts with auditees?
flashcards.back: Focus on facts, not personalities. Listen to understand concerns. Explain audit perspective. Seek common ground. Escalate if necessary following defined process. Document disagreements. Maintain professional demeanor.

flashcards.front: What is the Internal Audit Capability Model (IA-CM)?
flashcards.back:
IIA framework for assessing internal audit maturity:
Level 1: Initial (ad hoc)
Level 2: Infrastructure (sustainable)
Level 3: Integrated (aligned with organization)
Level 4: Managed (quantitative)
Level 5: Optimizing (continuous improvement)

flashcards.front: How does internal audit demonstrate its value proposition?
flashcards.back: Quantify cost savings from recommendations implemented. Track risk issues identified before becoming problems. Measure stakeholder satisfaction. Report on contribution to governance, risk, and control improvements. Benchmark against peers.

flashcards.front: What is agile internal auditing?
flashcards.back: Applying agile principles to audit: iterative delivery, continuous feedback, flexible scope, shorter planning cycles, cross-functional collaboration, rapid response to emerging risks. Delivers value faster, increases responsiveness.

flashcards.front: What are key considerations for remote/virtual auditing?
flashcards.back: Technology tools for communication/collaboration, evidence gathering electronically, maintaining security/confidentiality, building rapport virtually, addressing observation challenges, managing time zones, cybersecurity of remote work.

flashcards.front: What are the IIA professional certifications?
flashcards.back:
CIA: Certified Internal Auditor (globally recognized)
CCSA: Certified in Control Self-Assessment
CGAP: Certified Government Auditing Professional
CFSA: Certified Financial Services Auditor
CRMA: Certification in Risk Management Assurance
QIAL: Qualification in Internal Audit Leadership

flashcards.front: What are the continuing professional education requirements for CIA?
flashcards.back: 40 hours per year required to maintain CIA certification. At least 2 hours must be ethics-related. Activities include conferences, seminars, self-study, teaching, writing articles, serving on committees.

flashcards.front: What should an auditor do if asked to participate in illegal or unethical activity?
flashcards.back: Decline to participate. Document the request. Report to appropriate level (supervisor, CAE, audit committee, ethics hotline). Consult with ethics/legal resources. Consider impact on continued employment and potential whistleblower protections.

flashcards.front: How should auditors handle gifts or entertainment from auditees?
flashcards.back: Follow organization"s gift policy. Decline gifts that could impair objectivity. Modest gifts (promotional items, meals) may be acceptable if customary and immaterial. Disclose significant gifts to supervisor. When in doubt, decline.

flashcards.front: What does "Conformance with the Standards" mean?
flashcards.back: Internal audit activity follows all applicable Attribute and Performance Standards. Limited deviations only for specific circumstances. Non-conformance disclosed when it impacts overall scope or operation. Demonstrated through QAIP.

flashcards.front: How is conformance with Standards documented?
flashcards.back: Internal audit charter, policies and procedures manual, engagement work programs, working papers, quality assurance reviews, external assessment reports, QAIP documentation, training records.

flashcards.front: How do risk appetite, risk tolerance, and risk capacity differ?
flashcards.back:
Risk Appetite: Amount of risk willing to accept in pursuit of objectives (strategic)
Risk Tolerance: Acceptable variation around objectives (tactical)
Risk Capacity: Maximum risk organization can absorb

Audit evaluates whether actual risks align with appetite.

flashcards.front: What is risk culture and why is it important?
flashcards.back: Shared values, beliefs, attitudes toward risk. Influences risk-taking behavior. Components: Tone at top, risk awareness, accountability, open communication, learning from failures. Internal audit assesses risk culture health.

flashcards.front: What regulatory requirements affect internal audit in financial services?
flashcards.back:
Banking: Basel requirements, OCC/Fed guidance
Insurance: Solvency II, state regulations
Securities: SEC requirements

Often require: Independent internal audit function, risk-based approach, board audit committee oversight, competent staff.

flashcards.front: How does internal audit differ in the public sector?
flashcards.back: Additional accountability to taxpayers/public. Yellow Book (GAGAS) requirements. Political environment considerations. Transparency requirements. Different governance structures. Budget constraints. Performance auditing emphasis.

flashcards.front: Why is emotional intelligence important for internal auditors?
flashcards.back: Build rapport with auditees, manage difficult conversations, perceive unspoken concerns, maintain composure under pressure, influence without authority, navigate organizational politics, lead high-performing teams.

flashcards.front: How can auditors influence without formal authority?
flashcards.back: Build credibility through expertise and reliability. Communicate persuasively. Understand stakeholder perspectives. Use data and evidence. Build relationships. Demonstrate value. Be a trusted advisor, not just evaluator.

flashcards.front: What should an internal audit strategic plan include?
flashcards.back: Vision and mission, alignment with organizational strategy, key objectives and metrics, resource requirements, technology roadmap, stakeholder engagement approach, talent development, continuous improvement initiatives, risk-based coverage approach.

flashcards.front: What metrics can measure internal audit effectiveness?
flashcards.back: Audit plan completion, findings by risk level, recommendations implemented, time to issue reports, stakeholder satisfaction, external assessment rating, staff certifications, audits per auditor, repeat findings rate, cost per audit hour.

flashcards.front: When did the Global Internal Audit Standards (GIAS) 2024 become effective?
flashcards.back: January 9, 2025. GIAS 2024 replaces the previous International Professional Practices Framework (IPPF) as the mandatory standards for internal auditors worldwide.

flashcards.front: What mnemonic helps you remember the 5 GIAS 2024 Domains?
flashcards.back:
PEGMP:
• P - Purpose of Internal Auditing
• E - Ethics and Professionalism
• G - Governing the Internal Audit Function
• M - Managing the Internal Audit Function
• P - Performing Internal Audit Services

flashcards.front: How many standards are in GIAS 2024, and how are they organized?
flashcards.back:
15 Standards organized into 5 Domains:
• Domain I: 1 Standard (Purpose)
• Domain II: 4 Standards (Ethics, Professionalism)
• Domain III: 2 Standards (Governance)
• Domain IV: 3 Standards (Management)
• Domain V: 5 Standards (Performing)

flashcards.front: How does GIAS 2024 structurally differ from the previous IPPF?
flashcards.back:
IPPF used:
• Attribute Standards (1000 series) - organizational requirements
• Performance Standards (2000 series) - engagement requirements

GIAS 2024 uses:
• 15 thematic standards in 5 domains
• Principles-based vs. rules-based
• Core Principles integrated into standards
• Implementation Guidance embedded within standards

flashcards.front: GIAS 2024 Domain I (Standard 1): Purpose of Internal Auditing
flashcards.back:
Standard 1 defines the purpose of internal audit:
• Provide independent assurance and advisory services
• Enhance governance, risk management, and control
• Support organizational objectives
• Defined in the internal audit charter

The Mission remains: "To enhance and protect organizational value by providing risk-based and objective assurance, advice, and insight."

flashcards.front: What standards are in GIAS 2024 Domain II: Ethics and Professionalism?
flashcards.back:
• Standard 2: Ethics and Professionalism (integrates Code of Ethics)
• Standard 3: Objectivity (individual-level objectivity)
• Standard 4: Independence and Objectivity (organizational independence)
• Standard 5: Competence (knowledge, skills, abilities)

flashcards.front: What happened to the IIA Code of Ethics under GIAS 2024?
flashcards.back:
The Code of Ethics is now INTEGRATED into Standard 2: Ethics and Professionalism.

The four principles remain:
• Integrity
• Objectivity
• Confidentiality
• Competency

They are no longer a separate mandatory document but are embedded in the standards framework.

flashcards.front: How does GIAS Standard 4 (Independence and Objectivity) map to the old IPPF?
flashcards.back:
GIAS Standard 4 replaces IPPF Standard 1100:

Old IPPF 1100: Independence and Objectivity
• 1110: Organizational Independence
• 1120: Individual Objectivity
• 1130: Impairment to Independence or Objectivity

New GIAS Standard 4:
• Covers both organizational independence and impairments
• Standard 3 separately addresses individual objectivity
• More integrated guidance on safeguards and threats

flashcards.front: What standards are in GIAS 2024 Domain III: Governing the Internal Audit Function?
flashcards.back:
• Standard 6: Roles and Responsibilities of the Board
  - Charter approval
  - CAE evaluation
  - Resource adequacy
  - QAIP oversight

• Standard 7: Chief Audit Executive Responsibilities
  - Strategic direction
  - QAIP establishment
  - Reporting relationships
  - Risk-based planning

flashcards.front: Where is the Quality Assurance and Improvement Program (QAIP) addressed in GIAS 2024?
flashcards.back:
QAIP is now addressed in Standard 7: CAE Responsibilities (previously IPPF Standard 1300).

The CAE must establish QAIP covering:
• Internal assessments (ongoing monitoring)
• External assessments (at least every 5 years)
• Reporting results to board
• "Conforms with the Standards" statement requirements

flashcards.front: What standards are in GIAS 2024 Domain IV: Managing the Internal Audit Function?
flashcards.back:
• Standard 8: Strategic Planning
  - Risk-based audit plan
  - Alignment with organizational objectives
  - Communication with board and management

• Standard 9: Resource Management
  - Staffing and competencies
  - Technology and methodology
  - Budget and resource allocation

• Standard 10: Coordination and Reliance
  - Combined assurance approach
  - Reliance on other assurance providers

flashcards.front: How does GIAS 2024 address combined assurance?
flashcards.back:
Standard 10: Coordination and Reliance specifically covers combined assurance:

• Identify and coordinate with other assurance providers
• Third parties, external audit, second line functions
• Determine appropriateness of reliance
• Reduce duplication and gaps in assurance coverage
• Map organizational assurance coverage

This is a more prominent focus than in previous IPPF.

flashcards.front: What standards are in GIAS 2024 Domain V: Performing Internal Audit Services?
flashcards.back:
• Standard 11: Planning Engagements
• Standard 12: Conducting Engagements (evidence gathering)
• Standard 13: Forming Conclusions (analysis and opinions)
• Standard 14: Communicating Results
• Standard 15: Monitoring Actions (follow-up)

These replace IPPF Performance Standards 2100-2600.

flashcards.front: How do GIAS Domain V standards map to old IPPF Performance Standards?
flashcards.back:
IPPF → GIAS Mapping:
• 2100 Nature of Work → Standards 11-12
• 2200 Engagement Planning → Standard 11
• 2300 Performing the Engagement → Standard 12
• 2400 Communicating Results → Standard 14
• 2500 Monitoring Progress → Standard 15
• 2600 Risk Acceptance → Standard 15

New: Standard 13 (Forming Conclusions) more explicitly addresses analysis and opinion development.

flashcards.front: Under GIAS 2024, when must the CAE escalate risk acceptance to the board?
flashcards.back:
Standard 15 (Monitoring Actions) requires:

The CAE must communicate to the board when management has accepted a level of risk that may be UNACCEPTABLE to the organization.

This preserves the previous IPPF 2600 requirement but places it within the context of ongoing monitoring and follow-up activities.

flashcards.front: How should CIA exam candidates approach the IPPF-to-GIAS transition?
flashcards.back:
IIA Guidance:
• Both IPPF and GIAS terminology may appear on exams during transition
• Focus on UNDERLYING CONCEPTS rather than specific framework labels
• Core principles and ethical requirements remain consistent
• Mission of Internal Audit is unchanged
• Know the crosswalk between old and new standard numbers

flashcards.front: What happened to the 10 Core Principles under GIAS 2024?
flashcards.back:
The 10 Core Principles are INTEGRATED into the 15 Standards rather than existing separately:

1. Integrity → Standards 2-3
2. Objectivity → Standards 3-4
3. Confidentiality → Standard 2
4. Competency → Standard 5
5. Independence → Standards 4, 6
6. Due Professional Care → Standards 5, 11-12
7. Quality → Standards 7, 9
8. Effective Communication → Standard 14
9. Insight/Foresight → Standards 12-13
10. Value → Standard 1

flashcards.front: What is the Mission of Internal Audit under GIAS 2024?
flashcards.back: "To enhance and protect organizational value by providing risk-based and objective assurance, advice, and insight."\n\nThis mission statement remains UNCHANGED from the IPPF, providing continuity through the transition to GIAS 2024.

flashcards.front: What is the primary benefit of GIAS 2024\
flashcards.back:
Benefits:
• Greater FLEXIBILITY in application across diverse organizations
• Adaptable to different sizes, industries, and contexts
• Focuses on professional judgment over rigid rules
• More applicable globally across regulatory environments
• Reduces prescription while maintaining professional standards

This contrasts with the more rules-based IPPF structure.

flashcards.front: How does GIAS 2024 handle implementation guidance compared to the IPPF?
flashcards.back:
IPPF used:
• Implementation Standards (.A for assurance, .C for consulting)
• Separate Practice Guides and Practice Advisories
• Supplemental Guidance documents

GIAS 2024 uses:
• Topical Requirements within each Standard
• Implementation Considerations embedded in Standards
• More integrated, consolidated guidance
• Less reliance on extensive separate documents

flashcards.front: The Three Lines Model - who does what?
flashcards.back:
THREE LINES:

1st Line: OPERATIONS
- Management and staff
- Own and manage risk
- Direct control activities

2nd Line: OVERSIGHT
- Risk management function
- Compliance, quality, IT security
- Expertise, monitoring, guidance

3rd Line: ASSURANCE
- Internal audit
- Independent, objective assurance
- Reports to governing body

Governing body oversees all three

flashcards.front: Attribute Standards 1000 series categories
flashcards.back:
P - Purpose, Authority, Responsibility (1000)
I - Independence & Objectivity (1100)
D - Due Professional Care (1200)
O - Quality Assurance & Improvement Program (1300)

Attribute standards address CHARACTERISTICS of organizations and individuals performing internal audit activities.

flashcards.front: Performance Standards 2000 series categories
flashcards.back:
M - Managing the IA Activity (2000)
P - Planning (2010)
E - Engagement Planning (2200)
N - Nature of Work (2100)
E - Engagement Performance (2300)
C - Communicating Results (2400)
C - Coordinating (2050)
M - Monitoring Progress (2500)

Performance standards describe the NATURE of internal audit activities.

flashcards.front: QAIP Internal and External Assessment Requirements
flashcards.back:
COIN = Continuous and Ongoing Internal assessments:
- Ongoing monitoring
- Internal reviews
- Periodic self-assessments

5E = Every 5 years External assessment:
- Qualified, independent assessor
- Full external assessment OR
- Self-assessment with independent validation

Results reported to board / governing body

flashcards.front: CAE dual reporting structure
flashcards.back:
FUNCTIONAL reporting to BOARD:
- Approve charter, risk-based plan
- Communications from CAE
- CAE appointment/removal
- Budget and resources
- Scope restrictions

ADMINISTRATIVE reporting to MANAGEMENT:
- Day-to-day operations
- Internal policies compliance
- HR matters
- Budget management

Functional = Independence | Administrative = Operations

flashcards.front: Fraud Triangle elements
flashcards.back:
Opportunity
- Weak controls
- Override capability
- Access to assets

Rationalization
- Justifying behavior
- "Everyone does it"
- "I deserve it"

Pressure (Motivation)
- Financial problems
- Lifestyle maintenance
- Performance targets

All three elements typically present in fraud

flashcards.front: COSO ERM Framework Components
flashcards.back:
Governance & Culture
Performance (Strategy & Objective-Setting)
Strategy & Objective-Setting

Risk Identification
Information, Communication & Reporting
Review & Revision

Monitoring

5 components, 20 principles in 2017 framework

flashcards.front: COSO Internal Control Framework Components
flashcards.back:
Control Environment (tone at top)
Risk Assessment (identify & analyze)
Information & Communication
Monitoring Activities
Existing Control Activities

5 components, 17 principles
Applicable to all organizations

flashcards.front: What are the steps in the risk assessment process?
flashcards.back:
1. **Identify** risks to objectives
2. **Analyze** risks (likelihood × impact)
3. **Evaluate** against risk appetite
4. **Prioritize** risks for treatment
5. **Respond** to risks (avoid, accept, reduce, share)
6. **Monitor** and review continuously

Document in risk register with owners assigned.

flashcards.front: What are the four risk response options? (TARA)
flashcards.back:
**T**erminate (Avoid) - Eliminate the activity causing risk

**A**ccept (Tolerate) - Accept risk within appetite, no action

**R**educe (Treat) - Implement controls to reduce likelihood/impact

**A**ssign (Transfer/Share) - Transfer to third party (insurance, outsourcing)

Choice depends on cost-benefit analysis and risk appetite.

flashcards.front: Distinguish between risk appetite and risk tolerance.
flashcards.back:
**Risk Appetite:** The amount and type of risk an organization is willing to accept in pursuit of its objectives. Board-level strategic decision.

**Risk Tolerance:** The acceptable variation from risk appetite for specific objectives. More operational and specific.

Example:
Appetite: "We accept moderate credit risk"
Tolerance: "Bad debt ratio not to exceed 3%"

flashcards.front: What are the components of the COSO ERM Framework?
flashcards.back:
**COSO ERM (2017) has 5 components and 20 principles:**

1. **Governance & Culture** - Sets tone, oversight structure
2. **Strategy & Objective-Setting** - Integrates risk into strategy
3. **Performance** - Identifies, assesses, prioritizes risks
4. **Review & Revision** - Evaluates ERM capabilities
5. **Information, Communication & Reporting** - Leverages information systems

Key theme: Risk integrated into strategy-setting.

flashcards.front: What are the 5 components of COSO Internal Control? (CRIME)
flashcards.back:
**C**ontrol Environment - Tone at top, ethics, oversight

**R**isk Assessment - Identify and analyze risks

**I**nformation & Communication - Relevant info flows

**M**onitoring - Ongoing and separate evaluations

**E**xisting Control Activities - Policies and procedures

*(Also: CE-RAICOM = Control Environment, Risk Assessment, Information & Communication, Control Activities, Monitoring)*

flashcards.front: What are the key elements of the control environment?
flashcards.back:
• **Integrity and ethical values** - Code of conduct, tone at top
• **Board independence and oversight** - Independent directors
• **Organizational structure** - Lines of authority
• **Commitment to competence** - Hiring, training, performance
• **Accountability** - Performance measures, incentives

Control environment is the FOUNDATION for all other components.

flashcards.front: What are common types of control activities?
flashcards.back:
• **Authorization** - Approval limits, delegation
• **Segregation of duties** - Incompatible duties separated
• **Verification** - Reconciliations, reviews
• **Physical controls** - Locks, access badges, inventory counts
• **IT controls** - Access controls, change management
• **Performance reviews** - Variance analysis, KPIs
• **Documentation** - Policies, procedures, audit trails

flashcards.front: Distinguish between IT general controls and application controls.
flashcards.back:
**IT General Controls (ITGCs):**
• Apply to all systems
• Access controls, change management
• Operations, backup, recovery
• System development controls
• Create environment for application controls

**Application Controls:**
• Specific to individual applications
• Input controls (validation, edits)
• Processing controls (calculations)
• Output controls (distribution, review)

ITGC weakness can undermine all application controls.

flashcards.front: What are the main types of audit procedures?
flashcards.back:
• **Inquiry** - Asking questions (least reliable alone)
• **Observation** - Watching processes in action
• **Inspection** - Examining documents/physical assets
• **Confirmation** - Independent verification from third party
• **Recalculation** - Re-performing calculations
• **Reperformance** - Independently executing procedures
• **Analytical procedures** - Evaluating relationships/trends
• **Testing controls** - Evaluating design and operating effectiveness

flashcards.front: What types of analytical procedures can auditors use?
flashcards.back:
**Trend Analysis** - Compare current to prior periods

**Ratio Analysis** - Calculate and compare ratios

**Reasonableness Tests** - Develop expectations, compare to actual

**Regression Analysis** - Statistical modeling of relationships

**Benchmarking** - Compare to industry or best practices

Useful for identifying unusual fluctuations or potential misstatements for further investigation.

flashcards.front: What is the Fraud Triangle?
flashcards.back:
Three conditions present when fraud occurs:

**1. Pressure/Incentive** - Financial difficulties, unrealistic targets

**2. Opportunity** - Weak controls, lack of oversight

**3. Rationalization** - Justification ("I deserve it," "temporary")

Internal auditors should be alert to red flags in all three areas during engagements.

flashcards.front: What are common fraud red flags?
flashcards.back:
**Behavioral:**
• Living beyond means
• Financial difficulties
• Unusually close vendor relationships
• Control issues, reluctance to share duties

**Documentary:**
• Missing documents
• Excessive voids or adjustments
• Photocopies substituted for originals
• Alterations or inconsistencies

**Analytical:**
• Unusual trends or transactions
• Large round-number transactions
• Period-end anomalies

flashcards.front: What are Computer-Assisted Audit Techniques (CAATs)?
flashcards.back:
CAATs use technology to perform audit procedures:

• **Test data** - Process fabricated transactions
• **Parallel simulation** - Re-process actual data
• **Embedded audit modules** - Continuous monitoring
• **Generalized audit software** - Extract, analyze data
• **Data analytics** - Pattern analysis, anomaly detection

Benefits: Test 100% of transactions, identify exceptions, continuous monitoring.

flashcards.front: What are the requirements for audit working papers?
flashcards.back:
**Purpose:** Support engagement conclusions and results

**Must include:**
• Planning documents and programs
• Analysis and evidence obtained
• Conclusions reached
• Recommendations

**Quality requirements:**
• Sufficient detail for experienced auditor to understand
• Clear, complete, organized
• Stand-alone (understandable without explanation)
• Cross-referenced and indexed

flashcards.front: What does engagement supervision require?
flashcards.back:
Supervision must ensure objectives are achieved, quality maintained:

• **Planning guidance** - Clear instructions and approach
• **Progress monitoring** - Track against plan
• **Issue escalation** - Timely resolution of problems
• **Work review** - Quality of evidence and conclusions
• **Team development** - Coaching and feedback

Extent of supervision depends on proficiency and experience of internal auditors.

flashcards.front: What is the focus of operational auditing?
flashcards.back:
Evaluates efficiency and effectiveness of operations:

**Efficiency:** Are resources used optimally?
(Minimizing waste, cost per unit, cycle time)

**Effectiveness:** Are objectives achieved?
(Quality, customer satisfaction, goal attainment)

**Key areas:**
• Business processes
• Resource utilization
• Compliance with policies
• Achievement of KPIs
• Value-added opportunities

flashcards.front: What does compliance auditing evaluate?
flashcards.back:
Assesses adherence to:

• **Laws and regulations** - Legal requirements
• **Contracts** - Vendor, customer, employee agreements
• **Policies and procedures** - Internal rules
• **Ethics** - Code of conduct compliance

**Key considerations:**
• Identify applicable requirements
• Test adherence through evidence
• Evaluate consequences of non-compliance
• Consider industry-specific regulations

flashcards.front: What are key tests in financial process auditing?
flashcards.back:
**Revenue Cycle:**
• Completeness of recorded revenue
• Cutoff testing
• AR confirmations

**Expenditure Cycle:**
• Authorization of purchases
• Three-way match
• Vendor validation

**Payroll:**
• Ghost employee testing
• Proper authorization
• Accurate calculations

**Treasury:**
• Bank reconciliations
• Investment authorization
• Cash handling

flashcards.front: What are key IT audit focus areas?
flashcards.back:
**IT Governance:** Alignment with business objectives

**Security:** Access controls, vulnerability management

**Change Management:** Controlled system changes

**Operations:** Backup, recovery, incident management

**Application Controls:** Input, processing, output controls

**Data Integrity:** Accuracy, completeness, validity

**Third-Party Risk:** Vendor management, cloud security

flashcards.front: What are special considerations for consulting engagements?
flashcards.back:
**Key differences from assurance:**
• Scope agreed with client
• May accept engagement if value added
• Can provide recommendations without full assessment

**Limitations:**
• Cannot assume management responsibility
• Cannot provide assurance on same area recently consulted
• Must maintain objectivity

**Examples:** Facilitation, training, advice, process design

flashcards.front: What factors should be considered when assessing engagement-level risk?
flashcards.back: Consider: 1) Significance of the area to organization, 2) Historical issues/problems, 3) Complexity of operations, 4) Changes since last review, 5) Competence of management, 6) Adequacy of control environment, 7) Results of prior audits, 8) External audit findings, 9) New regulations or requirements.

flashcards.front: What is the role of data analytics in engagement planning?
flashcards.back: Data analytics helps: 1) Identify risk areas, 2) Determine audit scope, 3) Design efficient tests, 4) Test entire populations vs samples, 5) Identify anomalies/exceptions, 6) Assess control effectiveness, 7) Provide continuous monitoring. Types include descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics.

flashcards.front: What are the requirements for engagement documentation (working papers)?
flashcards.back: Working papers must: 1) Document information sufficient to support conclusions, 2) Be complete, accurate, and objective, 3) Follow a consistent format, 4) Include audit objectives, scope, methodology, 5) Be reviewed appropriately, 6) Be controlled and secured, 7) Meet retention requirements. CAE must establish policies for custody and retention.

flashcards.front: What are effective inquiry techniques for internal auditors?
flashcards.back: Effective techniques: 1) Use open-ended questions (how, what, why), 2) Listen actively, 3) Corroborate responses with other evidence, 4) Interview multiple people, 5) Ask same question different ways, 6) Note non-verbal cues, 7) Document promptly, 8) Follow up on inconsistencies. Inquiry alone is rarely sufficient evidence.

flashcards.front: What are the main types of audit procedures (PSIRC)?
flashcards.back: PSIRC: Physical observation (seeing activities/assets), Systems walk-through (trace transactions), Inquiry (asking people), Reperformance (doing the control yourself), Confirmation (external verification). Also: Inspection of documents, Analytical procedures, Vouching/Tracing. Multiple procedures strengthen evidence.

flashcards.front: What are analytical procedures in auditing?
flashcards.back: Analytical procedures evaluate relationships among data to identify unusual patterns or anomalies. Types: 1) Trend analysis (year-over-year), 2) Ratio analysis, 3) Reasonableness tests, 4) Regression analysis, 5) Benchmarking. Used in planning, testing, and overall review phases. Develop expectations independently.

flashcards.front: When is non-statistical (judgmental) sampling appropriate?
flashcards.back: Appropriate when: 1) Population is small, 2) Auditor experience supports judgment, 3) Statistical precision not required, 4) Targeting specific risk areas, 5) Follow-up on specific issues. Disadvantages: Cannot project results to population, subjective, may not be representative. Results should not be extrapolated.

flashcards.front: What factors affect sample size in attribute sampling?
flashcards.back: Larger samples needed for: 1) Lower expected error rate (more sensitive), 2) Lower tolerable deviation rate, 3) Higher confidence level desired, 4) Larger population size (diminishing impact). Formula factors: Confidence factor, tolerable deviation rate, expected deviation rate. Risk of over-reliance = 100% - confidence level.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of testing automated controls?
flashcards.back: Advantages: 1) Consistent operation (programmed, no human error), 2) Process large volumes, 3) Can test 100% of transactions, 4) Once verified, operate consistently, 5) Provide strong control evidence. Testing: Verify program logic, test with known data, review change controls. Rely on ITGCs for ongoing operation.

flashcards.front: What are Computer Assisted Audit Techniques (CAATTs)?
flashcards.back: CAATTs are software tools used for: 1) Data extraction and analysis, 2) Duplicate detection, 3) Gap analysis (missing sequences), 4) Stratification and aging, 5) Benford\

flashcards.front: What are the components of access control (AAA)?
flashcards.back: AAA: Authentication (proving identity - who are you?), Authorization (granting access - what can you do?), Accountability (tracking actions - what did you do?). Also: Identification (claiming identity). Controls should enforce least privilege and segregation of duties.

flashcards.front: What are the criteria for communicating engagement results?
flashcards.back: Communications must be: 1) Accurate - free from errors, 2) Objective - fair, impartial, 3) Clear - easily understood, 4) Concise - to the point, 5) Constructive - helpful, 6) Complete - includes all relevant information, 7) Timely - issued without delay. Use the 7 C\

flashcards.front: What is the purpose and procedure for an exit conference?
flashcards.back: Purpose: 1) Discuss findings with management, 2) Obtain management responses, 3) Ensure accuracy of facts, 4) Agree on corrective actions, 5) Avoid surprises in the final report. Procedure: Present findings, listen to management views, document agreements, obtain commitments with dates.

flashcards.front: What is root cause analysis and why is it important?
flashcards.back: Root cause analysis identifies the fundamental reason why a problem occurred, not just symptoms. Techniques: 5 Whys, Fishbone/Ishikawa diagram, Fault tree analysis. Importance: 1) Prevents recurrence, 2) Leads to sustainable solutions, 3) Addresses systemic issues, 4) More valuable recommendations.

flashcards.front: What are the typical sections of an internal audit report?
