Futures: Standardized exchange-traded forwards
Options: Right (not obligation) to buy/sell
Swaps: Exchange of cash flows (interest rate, currency)

Used for hedging or speculation.

flashcards.front: What is the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) model?
flashcards.back:
EOQ = √(2DS/H)

D = Annual demand
S = Ordering cost per order
H = Holding cost per unit per year

Minimizes total inventory costs (ordering + holding). Assumes constant demand, instant delivery.

flashcards.front: What is the ASC 606 revenue recognition model?
flashcards.back:
5 Steps:
1. Identify contract with customer
2. Identify performance obligations
3. Determine transaction price
4. Allocate price to obligations
5. Recognize revenue when obligation satisfied

Revenue recognized when control transfers to customer.

flashcards.front: How are leases accounted for under ASC 842?
flashcards.back:
Lessees recognize right-of-use (ROU) asset and lease liability for most leases.

Finance Lease: Front-loaded expense (interest + amortization)
Operating Lease: Straight-line expense

Short-term leases (<12 months) may be expensed.

flashcards.front: How is asset impairment assessed and recorded?
flashcards.back: Long-lived assets: Test when events indicate impairment. Compare carrying value to undiscounted cash flows. If impaired, write down to fair value.\n\nGoodwill: Annual test. Compare carrying value to fair value of reporting unit.

flashcards.front: How are contingent liabilities accounted for?
flashcards.back:
Probable + Estimable: Recognize liability and expense
Probable + Not Estimable: Disclose in notes
Reasonably Possible: Disclose in notes
Remote: No recognition or disclosure required

Examples: Lawsuits, warranties, environmental cleanup.

flashcards.front: What are key differences between US GAAP and IFRS?
flashcards.back:
LIFO: Allowed under GAAP, prohibited under IFRS
Development costs: Expense under GAAP, capitalize if criteria met under IFRS
Revaluations: IFRS allows upward, GAAP does not
Principles vs Rules: IFRS more principles-based

flashcards.front: What are the key components of supply chain management?
flashcards.back: Sourcing/Procurement, Manufacturing/Production, Warehouse/Inventory, Logistics/Distribution, Demand Planning, Supplier Relationship Management, Customer Fulfillment\n\nGoal: Right product, right place, right time, right cost.

flashcards.front: What is Just-in-Time (JIT) inventory management?
flashcards.back:
Inventory arrives when needed for production, minimizing holding costs.

Benefits: Lower inventory, reduced waste, improved quality
Risks: Supply disruption impact, requires reliable suppliers, demand accuracy critical

flashcards.front: What are the risks and benefits of outsourcing?
flashcards.back: Benefits: Cost reduction, access to expertise, focus on core competencies, scalability\n\nRisks: Loss of control, quality issues, dependency on vendor, intellectual property, reputational risk, hidden costs

flashcards.front: What are the phases of project management?
flashcards.back:
Initiation: Define project, obtain authorization
Planning: Scope, schedule, budget, resources
Execution: Perform work, manage team
Monitoring & Control: Track progress, manage changes
Closure: Finalize, document lessons learned

flashcards.front: What is Agile methodology?
flashcards.back:
Iterative approach emphasizing flexibility, collaboration, continuous delivery.

Principles: Working software > documentation, customer collaboration > contracts, responding to change > following plan

Scrum, Kanban are common frameworks.

flashcards.front: What are key controls in the hiring process?
flashcards.back: Background checks, reference verification, credential validation, drug testing (where legal), interview documentation, non-discrimination compliance, job offer approval, onboarding process, I-9 verification.

flashcards.front: What are key elements of compensation and benefits?
flashcards.back: Base pay, variable pay (bonus, commission), equity/stock, benefits (health, retirement, PTO), perquisites.\n\nControls: Approval levels, market benchmarking, documentation, compliance with regulations, segregation of duties.

flashcards.front: What are the elements of effective performance management?
flashcards.back: Goal setting (SMART), ongoing feedback, regular reviews, documentation, development plans, calibration sessions, merit/compensation linkage, addressing poor performance, recognition programs.

flashcards.front: What are key employment laws in the US?
flashcards.back:
FLSA: Minimum wage, overtime, child labor
Title VII: Discrimination
ADA: Disability accommodations
ADEA: Age discrimination
FMLA: Family/medical leave
OSHA: Workplace safety

flashcards.front: What are the elements of effective training programs?
flashcards.back: Needs assessment, learning objectives, appropriate methods, qualified instructors, participant engagement, evaluation/feedback, documentation, application to job, measurement of effectiveness, ongoing development.

flashcards.front: What are the 4Ps of marketing?
flashcards.back:
Product: Features, quality, design, packaging
Price: Pricing strategy, discounts, payment terms
Place: Distribution channels, locations, logistics
Promotion: Advertising, sales, public relations, digital

Extended to 7Ps: Add People, Process, Physical Evidence.

flashcards.front: What is market segmentation?
flashcards.back:
Dividing market into distinct groups with similar needs.

Bases: Demographic (age, income), Geographic (location), Psychographic (lifestyle), Behavioral (usage, loyalty)

Targeting: Select segments to pursue
Positioning: Differentiate offering.

flashcards.front: What are the stages of the product lifecycle?
flashcards.back:
Introduction: Launch, slow sales, high costs
Growth: Rapid sales increase, competition enters
Maturity: Sales plateau, price competition
Decline: Sales decrease, decide to harvest or exit

Strategies differ for each stage.

flashcards.front: What are key customer metrics?
flashcards.back:
Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) = Marketing Spend / New Customers
Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) = Avg Revenue × Retention Period
Churn Rate = Lost Customers / Total Customers
Net Promoter Score (NPS) = Promoters % - Detractors %

flashcards.front: What are key digital marketing channels?
flashcards.back: Search (SEO, SEM/PPC), Social media, Email, Content marketing, Display advertising, Affiliate marketing, Mobile, Video\n\nMetrics: Click-through rate, conversion rate, cost per click, ROI, engagement rate.

flashcards.front: What are the main forms of international business?
flashcards.back:
Importing/Exporting: Least commitment
Licensing/Franchising: Rights transfer
Joint Ventures: Shared ownership
Wholly-Owned Subsidiaries: Full control, high risk
Strategic Alliances: Collaboration without equity

flashcards.front: What types of foreign exchange risk do companies face?
flashcards.back:
Transaction Risk: Exchange rate changes on outstanding transactions
Translation Risk: Effect on consolidated financials
Economic Risk: Long-term competitive position impact

Hedging tools: Forward contracts, options, natural hedges.

flashcards.front: What are Hofstede\
flashcards.back:
Power Distance: Acceptance of hierarchy
Individualism vs Collectivism
Masculinity vs Femininity: Competition vs cooperation
Uncertainty Avoidance: Tolerance for ambiguity
Long-term vs Short-term Orientation
Indulgence vs Restraint

flashcards.front: What are examples of international trade agreements and organizations?
flashcards.back:
WTO: Global trade rules
UsMCA: US-Mexico-Canada
EU: European Union single market
ASEAN: Southeast Asian cooperation

Agreements reduce tariffs, standardize regulations, facilitate trade.

flashcards.front: What is transfer pricing and why is it significant?
flashcards.back: Pricing of transactions between related entities (subsidiaries). Must follow arm\

flashcards.front: What are the cloud computing service models?
flashcards.back:
IaaS (Infrastructure): Virtual machines, storage, networks
PaaS (Platform): Development environment
SaaS (Software): Applications

Deployment: Public, Private, Hybrid, Community

Risks: Data security, vendor lock-in, availability.

flashcards.front: What are common cybersecurity threats?
flashcards.back:
Malware: Viruses, ransomware, trojans
Phishing: Social engineering emails
DDoS: Overwhelming systems
Man-in-the-Middle: Intercepting communications
Insider Threats: Employee/contractor misuse
APT: Sophisticated persistent attacks

flashcards.front: What are the key components of business continuity planning?
flashcards.back:
Business Impact Analysis (BIA): Identify critical functions
Risk Assessment: Threats and vulnerabilities
Recovery Strategies: Alternatives
Plan Development: Procedures
Testing: Exercises and drills
Maintenance: Regular updates

flashcards.front: What are RTO and RPO in disaster recovery?
flashcards.back:
RTO (Recovery Time Objective): Maximum acceptable downtime before operations restored

RPO (Recovery Point Objective): Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time

Drive backup frequency and recovery strategy selection.

flashcards.front: What emerging technologies impact internal auditing?
flashcards.back:
AI/Machine Learning: Advanced analytics, automation
Blockchain: Immutable records, smart contracts
RPA: Process automation
IoT: Connected devices, data volume
Big Data: Enhanced analytics capabilities

Auditors must understand risks and capabilities.

flashcards.front: What is capital structure and why does it matter?
flashcards.back:
Mix of debt and equity used to finance operations.

Tradeoffs:
Debt: Tax-deductible interest, but increases risk
Equity: No required payments, but dilutes ownership

Optimal structure minimizes WACC, maximizes firm value.

flashcards.front: What factors influence dividend policy?
flashcards.back: Profitability and cash flow, investment opportunities, debt covenants, legal restrictions, shareholder preferences, tax considerations, stock price effects, signaling effects, industry practices.

flashcards.front: What are the key considerations in mergers and acquisitions?
flashcards.back: Strategic fit and synergies, due diligence, valuation, deal structure (stock vs. cash), financing, regulatory approval, integration planning, cultural fit, retention of key personnel, post-merger integration.

flashcards.front: What are common business valuation methods?
flashcards.back:
DCF: Present value of future cash flows
Comparable Companies: Multiples from similar public companies
Precedent Transactions: Multiples from similar M&A deals
Asset-Based: Net asset value

Multiples: EV/EBITDA, P/E, EV/Revenue.

flashcards.front: What is ESG and why is it important?
flashcards.back:
Environmental: Climate, emissions, resources
Social: Labor practices, community, diversity
Governance: Board, ethics, transparency

Increasing investor focus, regulatory requirements, stakeholder expectations. Internal audit role in assessing ESG risks and disclosures.

flashcards.front: What are key audit areas in the banking industry?
flashcards.back: Credit risk (loan underwriting, portfolio quality), market risk, operational risk, regulatory compliance (BSA/AML, fair lending), capital adequacy, liquidity, IT security, vendor management, interest rate risk, fiduciary responsibilities.

flashcards.front: What are key audit areas in healthcare?
flashcards.back: Billing and revenue cycle, HIPAA compliance, patient safety, clinical quality, pharmaceutical controls, medical records, supply chain, credentialing, fraud (kickbacks, false claims), IT security, regulatory compliance.

flashcards.front: What are key audit areas in manufacturing?
flashcards.back: Inventory management, production efficiency, quality control, safety and environmental, supply chain, cost accounting, capital projects, maintenance, product liability, regulatory compliance, lean operations.

flashcards.front: What are key audit areas in retail?
flashcards.back: Inventory shrinkage, cash handling, POS systems, e-commerce, supply chain, vendor agreements, merchandising, store operations, customer data protection, gift card fraud, return fraud, labor compliance.

flashcards.front: What are key audit areas in insurance?
flashcards.back: Underwriting discipline, claims processing, reserving, reinsurance, agent/broker controls, policy administration, regulatory compliance, investment management, actuarial processes, fraud detection.

flashcards.front: What are the essential elements of a valid contract?
flashcards.back:
Offer: Proposal to enter agreement
Acceptance: Agreement to terms
Consideration: Exchange of value
Capacity: Legal ability to contract
Legality: Lawful purpose

Contracts may be written, oral, or implied.

flashcards.front: What contract risks should internal audit evaluate?
flashcards.back: Payment terms, indemnification clauses, limitation of liability, termination provisions, intellectual property, confidentiality, compliance requirements, insurance requirements, force majeure, dispute resolution, audit rights.

flashcards.front: What are the types of intellectual property?
flashcards.back:
Patents: Inventions (20 years)
Copyrights: Creative works (author life + 70 years)
Trademarks: Brand identifiers (renewable)
Trade Secrets: Confidential business info

IP protection critical for competitive advantage.

flashcards.front: What should internal audit understand about litigation?
flashcards.back: Document retention/legal hold requirements, discovery process, attorney-client privilege, work product doctrine, reporting of material litigation, reserves for probable losses, impact on audit access and timing.

flashcards.front: What are common corporate/business structures?
flashcards.back:
Sole Proprietorship: Individual owner, unlimited liability
Partnership: Shared ownership
Corporation: Separate legal entity, limited liability
LLC: Hybrid flexibility
S-Corp: Pass-through taxation

Structure affects liability, taxes, governance.

flashcards.front: What are the classifications of financial instruments?
flashcards.back:
Equity: Ownership (stocks)
Debt: Loans, bonds
Derivatives: Value from underlying asset
Hybrids: Convertible bonds, preferred stock

Classification affects accounting treatment, risk exposure, and disclosure.

flashcards.front: How is credit risk assessed and managed?
flashcards.back: Assessment: Credit scores, financial analysis, industry risk, collateral\nManagement: Credit limits, monitoring, diversification, insurance, guarantees\nMetrics: Days outstanding, aging, bad debt ratio, concentration

flashcards.front: What is interest rate risk and how is it managed?
flashcards.back:
Risk that rate changes affect asset/liability values or cash flows.

Management: Gap analysis, duration matching, interest rate swaps, caps/floors, repricing strategies

Important for banks, treasury, real estate.

flashcards.front: How are bonds valued?
flashcards.back:
Bond Value = PV of coupon payments + PV of face value

Price and yield move inversely. Duration measures price sensitivity to rate changes. Longer duration = more interest rate risk.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of leasing vs. buying?
flashcards.back: Leasing: Preserves capital, off-balance-sheet (operating), flexibility, tax benefits, transfers obsolescence risk\n\nBuying: Ownership, potential appreciation, no use restrictions, may be cheaper long-term

flashcards.front: What are key concepts in corporate taxation?
flashcards.back: Taxable income vs book income (permanent/temporary differences), deferred tax assets/liabilities, tax credits, NOL carryforwards, transfer pricing, state/local taxes, international tax considerations.

flashcards.front: What creates deferred tax assets and liabilities?
flashcards.back:
Timing differences between book and tax treatment:

Deferred Tax Liability: Tax paid later (accelerated depreciation)
Deferred Tax Asset: Tax benefit later (warranty accrual, NOL carryforward)

Recorded at enacted tax rates.

flashcards.front: What tax risks should internal audit consider?
flashcards.back: Compliance risks (filing, payments), uncertain tax positions, transfer pricing challenges, tax controversy, tax planning aggressiveness, legislative changes, jurisdiction risks, documentation requirements.

flashcards.front: How do fixed, variable, and mixed costs behave?
flashcards.back:
Fixed: Constant total, decreases per unit as volume increases
Variable: Constant per unit, increases total with volume
Mixed (semi-variable): Fixed component plus variable component

Understanding behavior essential for analysis.

flashcards.front: What is contribution margin and how is it used?
flashcards.back:
CM per unit = Price - Variable Cost per unit
CM Ratio = CM / Revenue

Uses: Break-even analysis, pricing decisions, product mix decisions, profitability analysis. Higher CM = more profit contribution per sale.

flashcards.front: What is Activity-Based Costing (ABC)?
flashcards.back: Assigns costs based on activities that drive costs. Identifies cost drivers for each activity. More accurate than traditional costing for complex operations. Steps: Identify activities, assign costs, identify drivers, allocate to products.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between absorption and variable costing?
flashcards.back:
Absorption: All manufacturing costs (fixed + variable) in inventory
Variable: Only variable costs in inventory, fixed costs expensed

Absorption required for GAAP. Variable useful for decision-making. Affects inventory valuation and timing of expense.

flashcards.front: What methods are used for internal transfer pricing?
flashcards.back:
Market-based: External market price
Cost-based: Cost plus markup
Negotiated: Agreement between divisions

Considerations: Goal congruence, motivation, tax implications, divisional performance evaluation.

flashcards.front: What are key descriptive statistics?
flashcards.back:
Mean: Average
Median: Middle value (better for skewed data)
Mode: Most frequent
Range: Max - Min
Standard Deviation: Measure of dispersion
Variance: SD squared

flashcards.front: What is regression analysis?
flashcards.back: Statistical method to model relationship between variables. Simple regression: One independent variable. Multiple regression: Multiple predictors. Uses: Forecasting, trend analysis, identifying relationships. R² measures explanatory power.

flashcards.front: How are decision trees used in business decisions?
flashcards.back: Visualize decision alternatives and outcomes. Nodes represent decisions or chance events. Branches show options and probabilities. Calculate expected value of each path. Choose option with best expected outcome.

flashcards.front: What is sensitivity analysis?
flashcards.back: Tests how changes in assumptions affect outcomes. Identify which variables have greatest impact. Stress testing for extreme scenarios. Helps understand risk and uncertainty in projections. Common in capital budgeting and forecasting.

flashcards.front: What is Monte Carlo simulation?
flashcards.back: Uses random sampling to model uncertainty. Runs many iterations with different input values. Produces probability distribution of outcomes. Useful for complex risk analysis, project management, financial modeling.

flashcards.front: What makes an effective Key Performance Indicator (KPI)?
flashcards.back: Aligned with strategic objectives, measurable and quantifiable, actionable, timely, relevant to decision-makers, comparable over time, has target/benchmark, limited in number, balanced across perspectives.

flashcards.front: What are the types of benchmarking?
flashcards.back:
Internal: Compare units within organization
Competitive: Compare to direct competitors
Functional: Compare processes across industries
Generic: Compare to best practices anywhere

Process: Plan, collect data, analyze gaps, implement improvements.

flashcards.front: How is Return on Investment (ROI) calculated and used?
flashcards.back:
ROI = (Gain from Investment - Cost) / Cost × 100%

Pros: Simple, widely understood
Cons: Ignores time value, risk, non-financial factors

Can encourage short-term focus over long-term value.

flashcards.front: What are the principles of ISO 31000?
flashcards.back: Risk management should be: Integrated, structured, customized, inclusive, dynamic, based on best available information, considers human and cultural factors, enables continual improvement. Framework: Leadership commitment, integration, design, implementation, evaluation.

flashcards.front: What is operational risk and how is it managed?
flashcards.back: Risk of loss from inadequate or failed processes, people, systems, or external events.\n\nManagement: KRIs, loss data collection, risk control self-assessment (RCSA), scenario analysis, controls testing, incident management.

flashcards.front: What is strategic risk?
flashcards.back: Risk that strategy fails to achieve objectives or becomes obsolete. Sources: Market changes, competitive dynamics, technology disruption, regulatory shifts, reputation damage. Internal audit assesses strategic risk management process.

flashcards.front: What are key controls for e-commerce operations?
flashcards.back: Website security (SSL, WAF), payment card (PCI-DSS), customer data protection, order fulfillment accuracy, return/refund controls, inventory management, fraud detection, privacy compliance, accessibility.

flashcards.front: What risks arise from digital transformation?
flashcards.back: Technology implementation risks, change management challenges, skill gaps, cybersecurity exposure, legacy system integration, data quality/migration, vendor dependency, regulatory compliance, customer adoption, ROI realization.

flashcards.front: What are key controls in the procurement process?
flashcards.back: Approved vendor list, competitive bidding thresholds, requisition approval authority, segregation of duties, three-way match, receiving verification, independent AP, vendor master changes, contract compliance monitoring.

flashcards.front: What are key controls in treasury operations?
flashcards.back: Bank account authorization, wire transfer approvals, bank reconciliation, cash forecasting, investment policy compliance, derivative authorization, counterparty limits, segregation of duties, debt covenant monitoring.

flashcards.front: What are key controls in the financial close process?
flashcards.back: Close calendar and checklist, account reconciliations, journal entry approval, intercompany reconciliation, management review analytics, disclosure checklist, segregation of duties, access controls, variance analysis.

flashcards.front: What are key controls for capital projects?
flashcards.back: Authorization and approval levels, business case requirements, project accounting, change order controls, progress monitoring, milestone verification, contractor management, capitalization vs. expense policies, post-completion review.

flashcards.front: COBIT 2019 Governance System Principles
flashcards.back:
Provide stakeholder value
Holistic approach
Dynamic governance system
Governance distinct from management
Tailored to enterprise needs
End-to-end governance system

6 principles for a governance framework
40 governance and management objectives

flashcards.front: Key financial ratio categories
flashcards.back:
Liquidity (current, quick ratio)
Activity/Asset (turnover ratios)
Profitability (margins, ROE, ROA)
Solvency/Leverage (debt ratios)
Efficiency (operating metrics)

Each category reveals different aspects of financial health

flashcards.front: Information Security CIA Triad
flashcards.back:
Confidentiality
- Information accessed only by authorized
- Encryption, access controls

Integrity
- Accuracy and completeness
- Checksums, audit trails

Availability
- Accessible when needed
- Redundancy, BCP/DRP

Foundation of information security

flashcards.front: BCP/DRP key metrics
flashcards.back:
RTO (Recovery Time Objective):
- Maximum acceptable downtime
- "How fast must we recover?"

RPO (Recovery Point Objective):
- Maximum acceptable data loss
- "How much data can we lose?"

MTPD (Maximum Tolerable Period of Disruption):
- Absolute maximum downtime before permanent harm

flashcards.front: ADKAR Change Management Model
flashcards.back:
Awareness (of need for change)
Desire (to support change)
Knowledge (how to change)
Ability (to implement change)
Reinforcement (to sustain change)

Individual-focused change model
Prosci methodology

flashcards.front: Porter"s Five Forces Analysis
flashcards.back:
Threat of new entrants
Rivalry among existing competitors
Interloper products (substitutes)
Bargaining power of buyers
Entrant barriers / Supplier power

Analyzes industry attractiveness and competitive dynamics

flashcards.front: Six Sigma DMAIC phases
flashcards.back:
Define (problem, goals, scope)
Measure (current performance)
Analyze (root causes)
Improve (implement solutions)
Control (sustain improvements)

Data-driven quality improvement
3.4 defects per million target

flashcards.front: SDLC phases for audit
flashcards.back:
Requirements gathering
Analysis and design
Development/coding
Implementation/testing
User acceptance testing
Maintenance/support

Audit focus: Controls at each phase
Agile: Iterative approach to same phases
